title: Principles of Human Genetics
section: Genetics, Aging, and Frontiers in Medicine

items.subtopic: Principles of Human Genetics | IMPACT OF GENETICS AND GENOMICS ON MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 1)
items.title: Role of Genomics in Personalized Medicine
items.content: Genomics plays a crucial role in personalizing medical decisions, optimizing drug therapy based on genetic characteristics, and predicting treatment efficacy and adverse events.
keyPoints: Precision medicine customizes medical decisions to individual patients.
Pharmacogenomics uses genotype to optimize drug therapy and predict outcomes.
Driver mutations or overexpressed signaling molecules in malignancies guide targeted therapies.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · Principles of Human Genetics | IMPACT OF GENETICS AND GENOMICS ON MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Principles of Human Genetics | IMPACT OF GENETICS AND GENOMICS ON MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 1)
items.title: Genetics and Common Complex Diseases
items.content: While genetics traditionally focused on rare single-gene diseases, it now encompasses common complex disorders influenced by multiple genes and environmental factors.
keyPoints: ~10% of pediatric admissions and childhood mortality are due to single-gene disorders.
Common conditions like hypertension and diabetes have significant genetic components.
GWAS elucidate disease-associated loci for polygenic disorders.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · Principles of Human Genetics | IMPACT OF GENETICS AND GENOMICS ON MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Principles of Human Genetics | IMPACT OF GENETICS AND GENOMICS ON MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 1)
items.title: Genomic Insights in Cancer Therapy
items.content: Cancer genomics has revolutionized cancer biology, emphasizing the importance of genomic profiles over tumor origin for therapy selection.
keyPoints: Cancer results from somatic mutations and can have hereditary predispositions.
The TCGA initiative characterizes genomic landscapes across >30 malignancies.
Genomic profiles guide targeted therapies more than tumor location.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · Principles of Human Genetics | IMPACT OF GENETICS AND GENOMICS ON MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 1)

items.subtopic: IMPACT OF GENETICS AND GENOMICS ON MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 2)
items.title: Role of Molecular Genetics in Pathogen Detection
items.content: Molecular genetics significantly enhances the detection and identification of infectious pathogens, especially those that are difficult to culture such as mycobacteria, viruses, and parasites. This approach improves diagnostic testing feasibility and accuracy.
keyPoints: Used clinically for identifying hard-to-culture agents like mycobacteria, viruses, and parasites.
Molecular genetics enhances the accuracy of diagnostic tests.
Facilitates tracking of infectious agents on local and global scales.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · IMPACT OF GENETICS AND GENOMICS ON MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 2)

items.subtopic: IMPACT OF GENETICS AND GENOMICS ON MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 2)
items.title: Genomic Data Integration into Clinical Practice
items.content: The integration of genomic data into electronic health records (EHRs) optimizes patient care by providing easy access to extensive genetic information. This facilitates the use of bioinformatics and big data analytics in clinical settings.
keyPoints: Genomic data can be integrated into EHRs, impacting clinical practice.
Access to databases like OMIM provides comprehensive information on monogenic disorders.
Bioinformatics and big data analytics aid in managing large volumes of genetic data.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · IMPACT OF GENETICS AND GENOMICS ON MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 2)

items.title: Understanding the Scale and Structure of the Human Genome
items.content: The human genome, consisting of approximately 3 billion base pairs per haploid set, is significantly larger than that of simpler organisms like E. coli. This vast amount of DNA is organized into 23 chromosomes, including autosomes and sex chromosomes, which are crucial for genetic diversity and inheritance.
keyPoints: Human genome contains ~3 billion base pairs (bp) per haploid set.
Genome size comparison: Human DNA is nearly 1000-fold greater than E. coli's genome.
23 chromosomes in humans: 22 autosomes and X, Y sex chromosomes.
Diploid cells have two sets of each chromosome; germ cells are haploid.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · THE HUMAN GENOME (part 1)

items.title: DNA Structure and Genetic Information Transmission
items.content: DNA's double-helix structure, composed of four bases (A, T, G, C), allows for stable replication and transmission of genetic information. The base-pairing mechanism ensures accurate DNA replication and transcription into RNA, which is then translated into proteins.
keyPoints: DNA is a double-stranded helix with bases A, T, G, C.
Base pairing: A-T, G-C via hydrogen bonds.
Genetic information flow: DNA $ \rightarrow $ RNA $ \rightarrow $ protein.
~1% of human DNA codes for proteins; the rest has regulatory and structural roles.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · THE HUMAN GENOME (part 1)

items.title: Functional Diversity in Noncoding DNA
items.content: While only about 1% of the human genome encodes proteins, noncoding regions play essential roles in gene regulation, chromatin structure, and genomic stability. These include introns, regulatory elements, RNAs, centromeres, telomeres, and repetitive sequences.
keyPoints: ~1% of DNA is protein-coding; the rest has various functions.
Noncoding DNA includes introns, promoters, enhancers, silencers, insulators.
Functions: RNA generation (non-protein coding), centromere/telomere formation, chromatin structure.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · THE HUMAN GENOME (part 1)

items.title: Understanding Gene Complexity and Expression
items.content: Genes are functional units that encode RNA products, often translated into proteins. The complexity of genetic expression is not solely defined by the number of genes but also by the generation of multiple mRNA isoforms through splicing and posttranslational modifications.
keyPoints: Human genome contains roughly 20,000 protein-coding genes with an average of about four different coding transcripts per gene.
Exome constitutes only 1.14% of the genome.
There are close to 200,000 transcripts including thousands of noncoding RNAs like miRNA and lncRNA involved in various cellular processes.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · THE HUMAN GENOME (part 2)

items.title: Significance of Single-Nucleotide Polymorphisms (SNPs) in Genetic Variation
items.content: Single-nucleotide polymorphisms are the most common type of genetic variation, occurring every 100–300 bases and accounting for over 90% of all sequence variations. They play a crucial role in genetic heterogeneity and can impact disease risk.
keyPoints: Humans differ from each other at ~4 to 5 million sites compared to the reference genome.
SNPs are prevalent across human populations with more than 1 billion validated SNPs.
SNPs contribute to genetic diversity, influencing disease predisposition and responses to medications.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · THE HUMAN GENOME (part 2)

items.title: Role of Copy Number Variations in Genetic Diversity
items.content: Copy number variations involve large genomic regions that are duplicated or deleted, altering gene dosage. They contribute significantly to genetic diversity and can have detrimental effects if essential genes are impacted.
keyPoints: 5–10% of the genome can display CNVs.
0.4–0.8% of genomes differ in terms of CNVs between individuals.
De novo CNVs can occur even between monozygotic twins.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · THE HUMAN GENOME (part 2)

items.title: DNA Mismatch/Repair Disorders and Cancer Predisposition
items.content: Genetic abnormalities in DNA mismatch/repair mechanisms can lead to disorders such as xeroderma pigmentosum, Bloom's syndrome, ataxia telangiectasia, and hereditary nonpolyposis colon cancer (HNPCC). These conditions predispose individuals to neoplasia due to the rapid accumulation of additional mutations.
keyPoints: DNA mismatch/repair disorders include xeroderma pigmentosum, Bloom's syndrome, ataxia telangiectasia, and HNPCC.
These disorders strongly predispose to neoplasia because of the rapid acquisition of additional mutations.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · THE HUMAN GENOME (part 3)

items.title: Genetic Diversity Through Meiosis
items.content: Meiosis generates genetic diversity through recombination and independent segregation of chromosomes. During meiosis, there is an exchange of DNA between homologous paternal and maternal chromosomes, forming chiasmata. This process, along with random chromosome segregation, results in over 8 million possible combinations of chromosomes.
keyPoints: Meiosis involves two distinct steps of cell division reducing the chromosome number to haploid state (1n).
Recombination occurs more frequently in female meiosis than in male meiosis.
There are $2^{23} (>8$ million) possible combinations due to random segregation of 23 chromosomes.
Each gamete is genetically unique due to recombination and independent chromosome segregation.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · THE HUMAN GENOME (part 3)

items.title: Understanding Gene Expression Regulation by Transcription Factors
items.content: Gene expression is regulated at the transcriptional level by DNA-binding proteins known as transcription factors. These factors can activate or repress transcription, and most genes have multiple regulatory elements near their transcription start sites.
keyPoints: Most genes contain 15–20 discrete regulatory elements within 300 bp of the transcription start site.
Transcription factors provide a combinatorial code for gene regulation, interacting with ubiquitous and cell-specific factors.
Gene expression involves multiple steps beyond transcription factor binding, including mRNA processing and posttranslational modifications.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · REGULATION OF GENE EXPRESSION (part 1)

items.title: Role of Co-activators and Co-repressors in Gene Regulation
items.content: Transcription factors interact with co-activators and co-repressors to form large regulatory complexes, which are influenced by cell-signaling pathways. These interactions stabilize the basal transcription complex necessary for RNA polymerase activity.
keyPoints: Co-activators and co-repressors modulate the function of DNA-binding transcription factors.
Regulatory complexes can be controlled through phosphorylation, acetylation, sumoylation, and ubiquitination.
The basal transcription factor complex consists of >30 different proteins.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · REGULATION OF GENE EXPRESSION (part 1)

items.title: Epigenetic Mechanisms in Gene Expression
items.content: Epigenetics involves modifications to DNA and histones that affect gene expression without altering the primary DNA sequence. These changes can be heritable or influenced by environmental factors.
keyPoints: DNA methylation typically occurs at CpG dinucleotides, often silencing gene expression when present in promoter regions.
Histone modifications such as methylation and acetylation alter chromatin structure, influencing transcriptional activity.
Epigenetic changes contribute to the cell-specific transcriptional signature (transcriptome) and protein profile (proteome).
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · REGULATION OF GENE EXPRESSION (part 1)

items.title: X-Inactivation: Dosage Compensation Mechanism
items.content: X-inactivation is a process in females where one of the two X chromosomes is silenced to prevent overexpression of X-linked genes. This involves the Xist gene, which produces lncRNA that mediates silencing through methylation and histone modification.
keyPoints: Occurs randomly in each cell, with the choice being heritable across cell divisions.
~15% of X-chromosomal genes escape inactivation.
Involves high DNA methylation and low histone acetylation on the inactive X chromosome.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · REGULATION OF GENE EXPRESSION (part 2)

items.title: Genomic Imprinting: Parental Allele-Specific Expression
items.content: Genomic imprinting results in monoallelic expression of certain genes, with the pattern being heritable and tissue-specific. This is mediated by DNA methylation and is crucial for normal development.
keyPoints: Imprinted genes are expressed from only one parental allele.
Defects can lead to disorders like Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome, Silver-Russell syndrome, Angelman's syndrome, and Prader-Willi syndrome.
GNAS1 gene imprinting causes different phenotypes based on maternal or paternal transmission.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · REGULATION OF GENE EXPRESSION (part 2)

items.title: Epigenetic Alterations in Cancer
items.content: Cancer is characterized by changes in DNA methylation and histone modifications, leading to gene expression alterations. These epigenetic changes can be targeted therapeutically.
keyPoints: Involves both hypermethylation (silencing tumor-suppressor genes) and hypomethylation (genomic instability).
Epigenetic changes are reversible, offering therapeutic potential with demethylating agents and histone deacetylase inhibitors.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · REGULATION OF GENE EXPRESSION (part 2)

items.title: Understanding Genetic Variants and Their Implications
items.content: Genetic variants can occur in the germline or somatic cells, leading to different inheritance patterns. Germline mosaicism can result in some progeny inheriting a mutation while others do not, complicating inheritance pattern assessments.
keyPoints: Variants may be lethal, less deleterious, or confer an advantage.
Germline variants are transmitted to progeny; somatic variants affect only the individual.
Mosaicism results from variations during development, leading to tissues with different genetic constitutions.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Types and Consequences of Sequence Variants
items.content: Sequence variants range from large chromosomal alterations to single nucleotide changes. These can lead to different phenotypic outcomes depending on their location and effect on protein structure.
keyPoints: Variants include whole-genome changes like triploidy, deletions, insertions, and point mutations.
Missense mutations alter amino acids; frameshift mutations change the reading frame.
Mutations in regulatory sequences can affect gene transcription levels.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Hotspots and Susceptibility to Mutagenesis
items.content: Certain DNA sequences are more prone to mutations, such as successive pyrimidine residues and CpG dinucleotides. These hotspots can lead to common mutation types like C → T transitions.
keyPoints: Pyrimidine dimers form under UV light and may cause mutations if unrepaired.
CpG sites are prone to methylation-induced deamination, leading to C → T transitions.
Certain amino acid substitutions are overrepresented due to the genetic code's nature.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Understanding Mutation Rates and Their Implications
items.content: Mutation rates are crucial for understanding genetic diversity and disease transmission. Germline mutations, which occur in reproductive cells, have different implications compared to somatic mutations. The male germline is more susceptible to new point mutations due to the higher number of cell divisions during spermatogenesis.
keyPoints: Mutation rates are estimated at ~10^-10/bp per cell division.
Spermatogenesis involves ~30 divisions by puberty and 20 each year thereafter, increasing mutation probability with paternal age.
About 1 in 10 sperm carries a new deleterious mutation.
New mutations account for a significant fraction of autosomal dominant and X-linked disorders (~10^-5-10^-6/locus per generation).
Gonadal mosaicism can lead to transmission of new mutations.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 2)

items.title: Impact of Unequal Crossing-Over on Genetic Disorders
items.content: Unequal crossing-over during DNA recombination can lead to gene duplications or deletions, causing genetic disorders. This process is particularly relevant in gene clusters with multiple homologous sequences.
keyPoints: Unequal crossing-over can result in gene duplication or deletion.
Growth hormone (GH) gene deletions often involve unequal crossing-over.
Duplication of the PMP22 gene leads to type IA Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease.
Deletion of PMP22 causes hereditary neuropathy with liability to pressure palsies (HNPP).
Glucocorticoid-remediable aldosteronism (GRA) results from a gene fusion involving CYP11B2 and CYP11B1 due to unequal crossing-over.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 2)

items.title: Gene Conversion and Its Role in Genetic Variation
items.content: Gene conversion is a nonreciprocal exchange of genetic information that can alter gene function. This process often involves intergenic exchanges between genes and pseudogenes, leading to nucleotide substitutions.
keyPoints: Gene conversion involves the replacement of an internal portion of a gene by a homologous segment from another allele or locus.
It can result in identical short DNA segments on two chromosomes despite parental differences.
Nucleotide substitutions during gene conversion can alter gene function.
In disease states, gene conversion often occurs between a gene and a related pseudogene, such as the 21-hydroxylase gene (CYP21A2) and its adjacent nonfunctional pseudogene (CYP21A1P).
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 2)

items.title: Gene Conversion and Revertant Mosaicism
items.content: Gene conversion can lead to mutagenesis, as seen in CYP21A2 gene mutations linked to congenital adrenal hyperplasia. Mitotic gene conversion may explain revertant mosaicism, where inherited mutations are corrected in certain cells, such as in autosomal recessive generalized atrophic benign epidermolysis bullosa.
keyPoints: Gene conversion between CYP21A2 and CYP21A1P pseudogene can cause mutagenesis.
Mitotic gene conversion may lead to revertant mosaicism, correcting mutations in specific cells.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 3)

items.title: DNA Repair Defects and Neoplastic Disorders
items.content: Defective DNA repair mechanisms are crucial in neoplastic disorders. Xeroderma pigmentosum, caused by defects in nucleotide excision repair, leads to extreme UV sensitivity. Ataxia-telangiectasia involves ATM gene mutations affecting DNA repair and cell cycle checkpoints.
keyPoints: Xeroderma pigmentosum results from defects in DNA damage recognition or repair pathways, causing UV sensitivity.
Ataxia-telangiectasia is linked to ATM gene mutations, leading to neurodegeneration and increased radiation sensitivity.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 3)

items.title: Hereditary Nonpolyposis Colorectal Cancer (HNPCC) and Mismatch Repair
items.content: HNPCC, or Lynch syndrome, is caused by mutations in mismatch repair genes like MSH2 and MLH1. It leads to early-onset colon cancer and other malignancies. Genetic screening can identify at-risk families for early intervention.
keyPoints: HNPCC involves autosomal dominant transmission of colon cancer with onset before age 50.
Mutations in MMR genes (e.g., MSH2, MLH1) cause microsatellite instability and high mutation rates in colon cancer.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 3)

items.title: Understanding Gain-of-Function and Loss-of-Function Mutations
items.content: Gain-of-function mutations are typically dominant, leading to phenotypic changes with a single affected allele. In contrast, loss-of-function mutations are usually recessive, requiring homozygosity or compound heterozygosity for disease manifestation.
keyPoints: Gain-of-function mutations are often dominant and result in phenotypic alterations when one allele is affected.
Loss-of-function mutations are typically recessive, with affected individuals being homozygous or compound heterozygous.
Haploinsufficiency occurs when a single normal allele cannot maintain a normal phenotype.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 4)

items.title: Impact of Haploinsufficiency and Dominant-Negative Effects
items.content: Haploinsufficiency can lead to diseases by affecting transcription factors or rate-limiting enzymes, such as in porphyrias. Dominant-negative effects occur when a mutant allele interferes with the normal gene product, potentially through multimeric protein complex disruption, binding site occupation, or cytotoxicity.
keyPoints: Haploinsufficiency affects transcription factors and rate-limiting enzymes, leading to diseases like porphyrias.

Dominant-negative effects can disrupt multimeric protein complexes (e.g., osteogenesis imperfecta), occupy binding sites (e.g., thyroid hormone resistance β), or cause cytotoxicity (e.g., α1 antitrypsin deficiency).
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 4)

items.title: Genotype and Phenotype: Alleles, Genotypes, and Haplotypes
items.content: Phenotypes are observable traits defined by genotypes. Alleles are alternative forms of a gene, with polymorphic variants having varying effects on function. Common disease-associated allelic variants include GluVal (E6V) in β-globin and ΔF508 deletion in CFTR.
keyPoints: Phenotype refers to observable traits; genotype is the genetic information defining these traits.
Alleles are alternative gene forms, with some being polymorphic and others disease-causing (e.g., GluVal E6V in β-globin, ΔF508 deletion in CFTR).
Genotypes describe specific alleles at a locus, such as APOE3/4 or APOE4/4.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 4)

items.title: Haplotypes in Disease Association and Pharmacogenomics
items.content: Haplotypes, groups of alleles linked at a genomic locus, are instrumental for tracking genetic transmission within families and identifying recombination events. They help establish associations between specific HLA haplotypes and diseases like 21-hydroxylase deficiency, complement deficiency, hemochromatosis, ankylosing spondylitis (HLA-B27), and type 1 diabetes mellitus (HLA-DR4). This understanding aids in identifying susceptibility to autoimmune diseases and tailoring medical therapies.
keyPoints: Haplotypes are groups of alleles linked at a genomic locus.
Used for tracking genetic transmission and detecting recombination events.
Specific HLA haplotypes associated with diseases like 21-hydroxylase deficiency, complement deficiency, hemochromatosis, ankylosing spondylitis (HLA-B27), type 1 diabetes mellitus (HLA-DR4).
Helps in identifying susceptibility to autoimmune diseases and tailoring medical therapies.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 5)

items.title: Genotype-Phenotype Correlation in Genetic Disorders
items.content: The genotype-phenotype correlation describes how specific mutations lead to particular phenotypes. This is evident in diseases like von Hippel–Lindau disease and laminopathies, where the phenotype varies based on mutation location or type within a gene. Hierarchical cluster analysis can reveal genotype-phenotype correlations, aiding in understanding disease manifestations.
keyPoints: Genotype-phenotype correlation links specific mutations to phenotypes.
Phenotypes vary with mutation location/type (e.g., von Hippel–Lindau disease).
Laminopathies show multiple disorders from LMNA gene mutations, with phenotype variation based on mutation position.
Hierarchical cluster analysis aids in understanding genotype-phenotype correlations.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 5)

items.title: Allelic and Phenotypic Heterogeneity in Genetic Testing
items.content: Allelic heterogeneity refers to different mutations at the same locus causing similar phenotypes, as seen with $\beta$-globin mutations leading to $\beta$ thalassemia. This necessitates comprehensive genetic testing across entire loci due to ~2000 variants in genes like CFTR. Phenotypic heterogeneity occurs when different mutations in a gene cause varied phenotypes, exemplified by FGFR2 mutations resulting in Crouzon's or Pfeiffer's syndromes.
keyPoints: Allelic heterogeneity: Different mutations at the same locus causing similar phenotypes (e.g., $\beta$ thalassemia).
Comprehensive testing needed due to ~2000 variants in genes like CFTR.
Phenotypic heterogeneity: Different mutations in a gene cause varied phenotypes (e.g., FGFR2 mutations leading to Crouzon's or Pfeiffer's syndromes).
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 5)

items.title: Understanding Nonallelic Heterogeneity in Genetic Disorders
items.content: Nonallelic heterogeneity occurs when similar disease phenotypes result from mutations at different genetic loci. This is common in diseases like osteogenesis imperfecta and muscular dystrophy, where multiple genes can contribute to the phenotype.
keyPoints: Mutations in different genes (e.g., COL1A1 or COL1A2 for osteogenesis imperfecta) can lead to similar phenotypes due to their roles in the same pathway or complex.
Muscular dystrophy can result from mutations in various genes, including the X-linked DMD gene, which is large (2.3 MB, 79 exons).
Recognition of nonallelic heterogeneity complicates linkage studies and genetic testing but provides insights into molecular physiology.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 6)

items.title: Phenocopies: Nongenetic Mimics of Genetic Disorders
items.content: Phenocopies are conditions where nongenetic factors mimic genetic disorders, potentially confounding diagnosis and genetic testing. Examples include toxin-induced neurologic syndromes resembling Huntington's disease.
keyPoints: Nongenetic conditions can mimic genetic disorders, such as vascular causes of dementia mimicking familial Alzheimer's.
Phenocopies can complicate linkage studies and genetic testing.
Patient history and subtle phenotypic differences are crucial for distinguishing phenocopies from genetic conditions.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 6)

items.title: Variable Expressivity and Incomplete Penetrance in Genetic Disorders
items.content: Variable expressivity refers to the range of symptoms that can occur in different individuals with the same genetic mutation. Incomplete penetrance means not all individuals with a mutation show symptoms.
keyPoints: The same mutation can lead to varying phenotypes, as seen in multiple endocrine neoplasia (MEN) and cystic fibrosis.
Environmental factors, such as diet, can influence disease expression, e.g., iron intake affecting hemochromatosis.
Modifier genes and genetic background contribute to variable expressivity.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 6)

items.title: Understanding Penetrance in Genetic Disorders
items.content: Penetrance is the proportion of individuals with a mutant genotype who express the phenotype. Incomplete penetrance can result in skipped generations, as seen in dominant disorders like hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy (HCM). Age and imprinting can modify penetrance.
keyPoints: Complete penetrance means all carriers express the phenotype; incomplete or reduced penetrance means some do not.
Dominant conditions with incomplete penetrance may skip generations.
Age-specific penetrance is crucial for postnatal onset disorders like Huntington's disease and familial amyotrophic lateral sclerosis.
Imprinting affects penetrance, e.g., AHO mutations in GNAS1 gene are expressed only if inherited from the mother.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 7)

items.title: Sex-Influenced Phenotypes and Genetic Disorders
items.content: Mutations can affect males and females differently due to X-linked or Y-linked inheritance, or sex-limited expression. Examples include hemophilia A in heterozygous females and male-limited precocious puberty.
keyPoints: X-linked disorders express fully in males but variably in heterozygous females (e.g., hemophilia A).
Y-linked mutations affect only males, e.g., SRY gene causing sex reversal.
Sex-limited expression occurs due to differential gene function; e.g., activating mutations in the luteinizing hormone receptor cause male-limited precocious puberty.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 7)

items.title: Chromosomal Disorders and Their Prevalence
items.content: Chromosomal disorders arise from numerical or structural aberrations in chromosomes. They are prevalent in the general population, stillbirths, and spontaneously aborted fetuses.
keyPoints: Chromosomal disorders occur due to aneuploidy or structural changes like deletions and translocations.
Prevalence: ~1% in the general population, 8% in stillbirths, and close to 50% in spontaneously aborted fetuses.
Contiguous gene syndromes help identify new disease-causing genes.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 7)

items.title: Understanding Autosomal Dominant Inheritance
items.content: Autosomal dominant disorders occur when a mutation in one allele is sufficient to cause disease. These disorders manifest across successive generations, with an equal frequency of occurrence in males and females due to the autosomal nature of the gene involved.
keyPoints: 65% of human monogenic disorders are autosomal dominant.
Affected individuals have a 50% chance of passing the disorder to offspring if one parent is affected.
De novo germline mutations can occur, especially in older fathers (e.g., Marfan's syndrome average paternal age ~37 years).
Disease does not appear in offspring of unaffected individuals unless there is a new mutation.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 8)

items.title: Characteristics of Autosomal Recessive Disorders
items.content: Autosomal recessive disorders require homozygosity or compound heterozygosity for a defective gene to manifest. These conditions often involve metabolic pathways and are more common in populations with consanguineous marriages.
keyPoints: 25% of human monogenic disorders are autosomal recessive.
Affected individuals typically have two mutated alleles, either homozygous or compound heterozygous.
There is a 25% chance for offspring to be affected if both parents are heterozygous carriers.
Heterozygotes may show subtle phenotypic differences under certain conditions (e.g., sickle cell crises in heterozygotes during dehydration).
New mutations rarely manifest due to the asymptomatic carrier state.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 8)

items.title: Pedigree Analysis and Genetic Testing in Monogenic Disorders
items.content: Pedigree analysis is crucial for determining the mode of inheritance in monogenic disorders. Genetic testing aids in characterizing these disorders, which are cataloged in resources like OMIM.
keyPoints: Monogenic disorders follow Mendelian principles of inheritance.
Genetic testing plays a significant role in diagnosing and managing monogenic disorders.
OMIM provides comprehensive data on clinical phenotypes, molecular bases, and allelic variants for these disorders.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 8)

items.title: X-Linked Disorders: Transmission and Phenotypic Expression
items.content: In X-linked disorders, males are hemizygous for the mutant allele on their single X chromosome, leading to a higher likelihood of expressing the phenotype. Daughters of affected males are obligate carriers, inheriting the father's X chromosome with the mutation.
keyPoints: Absence of father-to-son transmission in X-linked disorders
All daughters of an affected male are obligate carriers
Males more likely to express mutant phenotype due to hemizygosity
Expression terms (dominant/recessive) apply only to women
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 9)

items.title: Y-Linked Disorders: SRY Gene and Sex Determination
items.content: The Y chromosome, particularly the SRY gene, is crucial for male development. Mutations or translocations involving the SRY can lead to sex reversal phenotypes such as XY females or XX males.
keyPoints: SRY gene on Y chromosome determines testis development
Crossing-over events can result in sex reversals (XY females, XX males)
Most SRY mutations are de novo
Microdeletions on Y chromosome linked to oligospermia/azoospermia
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 9)

items.title: Mitochondrial Disorders: Matrilineal Inheritance and Clinical Variability
items.content: Mitochondrial disorders are inherited maternally, with all offspring of an affected mother at risk. The variability in clinical presentation is due to heteroplasmia, where the proportion of mutant mitochondria varies among tissues.
keyPoints: Inherited through maternal line; not from father
Alterations affect oxidative phosphorylation and ATP production
Clinical spectrum includes myopathies and encephalopathies
Heteroplasmia causes phenotypic variability
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 9)

items.title: Mosaicism: Genetic Diversity Within Individuals
items.content: Mosaicism involves the presence of two or more genetically distinct cell lines within an individual, arising from mutations during embryonic, fetal, or extrauterine development. The timing and nature of these mutations determine whether germ cells and/or somatic cells are affected.
keyPoints: Chromosomal mosaicism results from nondisjunction at early embryonic mitotic divisions.
Somatic mosaicism leads to patchy distribution of genetically altered somatic cells.
McCune-Albright syndrome is an example, caused by postzygotic activating mutations in the G protein α (Gα) with variable clinical manifestations like ovarian cysts and polyostotic fibrous dysplasia.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 10)

items.title: X-Inactivation, Imprinting, and Uniparental Disomy: Parent-of-Origin Effects
items.content: Certain genetic disorders are influenced by the parental origin of genes due to mechanisms like X-inactivation, genomic imprinting, and uniparental disomy. These phenomena can lead to distinct syndromes depending on which parent transmits the mutation.
keyPoints: X-inactivation silences one X chromosome in females, affecting gene expression.
Prader-Willi syndrome results from deletions of paternal genes on chromosome 15 or uniparental disomy.
Angelman's syndrome arises from maternal deletions on chromosome 15 or uniparental disomy.
Imprinting defects can also lead to these syndromes, depending on the parent affected.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 10)

items.title: Genomic Imprinting and Disease: Beckwith-Wiedemann Syndrome
items.content: Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome (BWS) is associated with genomic imprinting, particularly involving the insulin-like growth factor 2 (IGF-2) gene. This condition leads to overgrowth and an increased risk of embryonal malignancies.
keyPoints: BWS involves somatic overgrowth and organomegaly, with a predisposition to Wilms' tumor.
Causes include paternal uniparental disomy of chromosome 11, aberrant methylation, or deletions affecting IGF-2 expression.
Normally, only the paternally derived IGF2 gene is active; BWS results from overactivity of IGF-2 or loss of CDKN1C function.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 10)

items.title: Cancer as a Genetic Disease and Multistep Carcinogenesis
items.content: Cancers are monoclonal, originating from a single cell with mutations in genes controlling growth or apoptosis. These somatic mutations lead to multistep carcinogenesis, requiring multiple genetic alterations for malignant progression.
keyPoints: Cancer is considered a genetic disease at the cellular level.
Monoclonal origin indicates a single precursor cell mutation.
Multistep carcinogenesis involves several genetic alterations.
Genome-wide analyses reveal somatic rearrangements and fusion genes.
Intratumoral, intermetastatic, and intrametastatic heterogeneity contribute to cancer evolution.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 11)

items.title: Telomerase Role in Cancer and Therapeutic Implications
items.content: Most human tumors express telomerase, which maintains telomere length and enhances replicative capacity. Telomerase inhibitors are a potential strategy for treating advanced cancers.
keyPoints: Telomeres protect chromosome ends from damage.
Telomere shortening occurs with age.
Telomerase adds repeats to chromosome ends during replication.
Most tumors express telomerase, aiding in cancer cell proliferation.
Telomerase inhibitors are a potential treatment for advanced cancers.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 11)

items.title: Knudson's Two-Hit Model and Inherited Cancer Syndromes
items.content: Inherited cancer syndromes often follow Knudson's two-hit model, where a germline mutation inactivates one allele of a tumor-suppressor gene. A second somatic event leads to neoplastic growth.
keyPoints: Knudson's two-hit model explains tumorigenesis in inherited cancer syndromes.
Germline mutations are autosomal dominant, leading to biallelic loss for tumor formation.
Examples include retinoblastoma, MEN 1, and neurofibromatosis types 1 and 2.
MEN 2 syndrome involves a gain-of-function mutation in the RET gene.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 11)

items.title: Understanding Polygenic and Multifactorial Disorders
items.content: Polygenic disorders involve multiple genes contributing to a phenotype, while multifactorial disorders result from gene-environment interactions. These complex traits require models that account for genetic heterogeneity and epistasis.
keyPoints: Traits are polygenic if multiple genes contribute to the phenotype.
Multifactorial traits involve gene-environment interactions.
Genetic heterogeneity and epistasis play crucial roles in these disorders.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 12)

items.title: Role of Genetic Studies in Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
items.content: Type 2 diabetes mellitus exemplifies a multifactorial disorder, with genetic, nutritional, and lifestyle factors intertwined. Identifying genetic variations and environmental factors is key for predicting disease risk and developing therapies.
keyPoints: Genetic, nutritional, and lifestyle factors are interrelated in type 2 diabetes pathogenesis.
Identification of genetic variations aids in predicting disease risk and designing preventive strategies.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 12)

items.title: Principles of Genetic Linkage
items.content: Genetic linkage refers to the physical connection of genes on chromosomes, affecting their transmission. The likelihood of recombination between linked genes is proportional to their distance.
keyPoints: Linked genes are usually transmitted together unless separated by a recombination event.
Recombination probability is proportional to the distance between genes.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 12)

items.title: Understanding Linkage Analysis for Disease Gene Identification
items.content: Linkage analysis involves assessing whether certain DNA marker alleles cosegregate with a disease within pedigrees. Markers closest to the disease gene are less likely to undergo recombination, thus receiving higher linkage scores.
keyPoints: Linkage is expressed as a lod (logarithm of odds) score.
A lod score of +3 supports linkage (1000:1 probability).
A lod score of -2 suggests absence of linkage.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 13)

items.title: Role of Allelic Association and Linkage Disequilibrium in Gene Mapping
items.content: Allelic association studies compare allele frequencies between affected individuals and controls to identify susceptibility genes. Linkage disequilibrium occurs when alleles at two loci are found together more frequently than expected, aiding in disease gene mapping.
keyPoints: Association studies can be case-control or family-based.
Linkage disequilibrium indicates tight linkage between loci.
Useful for identifying susceptibility genes in complex diseases.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 13)

items.title: Population Genetics and Allele Frequency Variations
items.content: Population genetics examines genotype distribution patterns. The Hardy-Weinberg equation ($ p^{2} + 2pq + q^{2} = 1 $) describes genotype frequencies for two alleles, A and a. Allele frequencies vary among populations due to selective advantages.
keyPoints: Genotype frequency formula: $ p^{2} + 2pq + q^{2} = 1 $.
Allele frequencies differ by ethnicity and geography.
Example: Sickle cell heterozygotes have malaria resistance.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 13)

items.title: Importance of Family History in Identifying Hereditary Predispositions
items.content: A comprehensive family history is crucial for recognizing potential hereditary predispositions to diseases. This involves documenting first-degree relatives, who share 50% of genes with the patient, and updating this information periodically due to age-dependent expressivity and penetrance.
keyPoints: First-degree relatives (parents, siblings, children) share 50% of genes with the patient.
Family history should include ethnic background, age, health status, and deaths.
Intermittent updates are necessary due to age-dependent expressivity and penetrance.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · Inherited Disorders

items.title: Considerations for Genetic Testing in Suspected Inherited Disorders
items.content: When a genetic disorder is suspected, identifying an appropriate laboratory for molecular diagnosis is essential. Genetic testing should be performed with informed consent due to potential psychological and social implications.
keyPoints: Genetic testing is available through commercial labs for many monogenic disorders; specialized labs may handle uncommon disorders.
Informed consent is required before testing, addressing implications like psychological distress and discrimination risks.
Negative or inconclusive results are possible due to technical limitations.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · Inherited Disorders

items.title: Role and Challenges of DNA Testing in Precision Medicine
items.content: DNA testing enhances medical care by identifying genetic predispositions, aiding pharmacotherapy selection, and enabling personalized treatment. It involves mutational analysis or linkage studies for at-risk individuals. Mass screening programs need high sensitivity and specificity to be cost-effective, with considerations around potential discrimination and harm.
keyPoints: Precision medicine uses genotypic analysis (DNA testing) for disease predisposition identification and pharmacotherapy selection.
Technical advances in RNA sequencing enhance genetic investigations by detecting gene fusions or aberrant expression patterns.
Mass screening requires high sensitivity and specificity; benefits include early intervention, while risks involve potential discrimination and harm.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · IDENTIFYING THE DISEASE-CAUSING GENE (part 1)

items.title: Techniques for Mutational Analysis
items.content: Mutational analysis is a key diagnostic tool using DNA sequence analysis to determine carrier status and perform prenatal testing. Techniques include PCR for rapid gene amplification, karyotype analysis, FISH, and NGS platforms for comprehensive sequencing of exomes or genomes.
keyPoints: DNA sequence analysis enhances diagnostic accuracy in monogenic disorders.
PCR allows genetic testing with small DNA samples from various sources like leukocytes or buccal cells.
NGS platforms enable unbiased, cost-effective characterization of rare disorders and malignancies.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · IDENTIFYING THE DISEASE-CAUSING GENE (part 1)

items.title: Challenges in Integrating Genomic Tests into Clinical Practice
items.content: Integrating genomic tests faces challenges such as variable test sensitivities, bioinformatics complexities, data storage and sharing, and interpreting genetic variants. Incidental findings can pose ethical dilemmas by revealing medically actionable conditions or unknown significance mutations.
keyPoints: Integration of genomic tests involves challenges like variable test sensitivity and complex bioinformatics analysis.
Incidental findings from comprehensive testing can reveal undiagnosed conditions or unknown significance mutations, posing ethical dilemmas.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · IDENTIFYING THE DISEASE-CAUSING GENE (part 1)

items.title: Approach to Mutational Analysis
items.content: A structured algorithm is essential for mutational analysis in suspected genetic disorders and malignancies. Detailed clinical phenotype characterization, consideration of genetic heterogeneity and phenocopies, and direct gene analysis if candidate genes are identified are crucial steps.
keyPoints: Detailed clinical phenotype characterization is critical.
Consider genetic heterogeneity and phenocopies.
Directly analyze suggested candidate genes.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · IDENTIFYING THE DISEASE-CAUSING GENE (part 2)

items.title: Prenatal Genetic Diagnosis
items.content: Various prenatal diagnostic techniques, including amniocentesis, chorionic villus sampling (CVS), and percutaneous umbilical blood sampling (PUBS), allow for the detection of genetic disorders. Each method has specific indications and timeframes.
keyPoints: Amniocentesis is performed at 16 weeks gestation; CVS can be done earlier, at 9–12 weeks.
Indications include advanced maternal age (>35 years), abnormal ultrasound findings, abnormal serum 'quad' test results, family history of chromosomal abnormalities, or Mendelian disorders.
Prenatal cfDNA allows non-invasive screening for certain chromosomal abnormalities and fetal sex.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · IDENTIFYING THE DISEASE-CAUSING GENE (part 2)

items.title: Gene Therapy and Genome Editing
items.content: Gene therapy, including CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing, offers potential treatments for various genetic disorders. Several gene transfer methods have been approved for clinical use, with recent FDA approvals for sickle cell disease.
keyPoints: Approved gene therapies exist for conditions like Leber congenital amaurosis and spinal muscular atrophy.
CRISPR-Cas9 is a promising approach for diseases such as cystic fibrosis and hemophilia.
First CRISPR-based therapies for sickle cell disease were approved by the FDA in 2023.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · IDENTIFYING THE DISEASE-CAUSING GENE (part 2)

items.title: Genetic Privacy and Discrimination: Understanding GINA and Its Limitations
items.content: The Genetic Information Nondiscrimination Act (GINA) was enacted in 2008 to protect asymptomatic individuals from genetic discrimination by insurers or employers. However, it does not extend protections to symptomatic individuals. The U.S. Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act of 2014 addresses some gaps by prohibiting health insurance exclusion based on personal health status.
keyPoints: Genetic information is sensitive and should be protected with explicit consent (genetic privacy).
GINA, enacted in 2008, protects asymptomatic individuals from genetic discrimination in health insurance and employment.
GINA does not protect symptomatic individuals.
The U.S. Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act of 2014 prohibits exclusion or termination of health insurance based on personal health status.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · ETHICAL ISSUES

items.title: Impacts of Genetic Testing: Benefits, Challenges, and Ethical Considerations
items.content: Genetic testing can significantly impact diagnosis, treatment, and prevention. While identifying mutations in conditions like MEN 2 or hemochromatosis allows for specific interventions, the identification of genes related to Alzheimer's or Huntington's disease does not currently alter therapy. Genetic test results may cause anxiety, especially with comprehensive sequence analyses that reveal multiple serious recessive gene mutations.
keyPoints: Identifying disease-causing genes can improve diagnosis, treatment, and prevention.
Mutations in MEN 2 or hemochromatosis allow specific interventions for family members.
Identification of Alzheimer's or Huntington's disease genes does not currently change therapy.
Comprehensive sequence analyses may reveal several serious recessive gene mutations, causing anxiety.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · ETHICAL ISSUES

items.subtopic: Principles of Human Genetics | IMPACT OF GENETICS AND GENOMICS ON MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 1)
items.question: What is the primary focus of traditional genetics?
options: Common complex diseases such as hypertension and diabetes
items.explanation: Traditional genetics has predominantly focused on chromosomal and metabolic disorders.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · Principles of Human Genetics | IMPACT OF GENETICS AND GENOMICS ON MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Principles of Human Genetics | IMPACT OF GENETICS AND GENOMICS ON MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 1)
items.question: What does precision medicine aim to achieve?
options: Developing universal treatments for all patients
Customizing medical decisions to an individual patient
items.explanation: Precision medicine aims at customizing medical decisions to an individual patient.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · Principles of Human Genetics | IMPACT OF GENETICS AND GENOMICS ON MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Principles of Human Genetics | IMPACT OF GENETICS AND GENOMICS ON MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 1)
items.question: Which initiative has characterized the genomic landscape across more than 30 malignancies?
items.explanation: The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) initiative has characterized the genomic landscape across >30 malignancies.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · Principles of Human Genetics | IMPACT OF GENETICS AND GENOMICS ON MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Principles of Human Genetics | IMPACT OF GENETICS AND GENOMICS ON MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 1)
items.question: What role do genome-wide polygenic risk scores (PRS) play?
options: They are used to diagnose single-gene disorders.
They may impact disease prevention in the future.
They identify driver mutations for targeted therapies.
items.explanation: Genome-wide polygenic risk scores (PRS) for common complex diseases are beginning to emerge and may impact disease prevention in the future.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · Principles of Human Genetics | IMPACT OF GENETICS AND GENOMICS ON MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 1)

items.subtopic: IMPACT OF GENETICS AND GENOMICS ON MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 2)
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a benefit of molecular genetics in medical diagnostics?
options: Improved feasibility and accuracy of diagnostic testing
Identification of agents difficult to culture
Development of new antibiotics for bacterial infections
Tracking infectious agents locally or globally
items.explanation: Molecular genetics aids in diagnostics but does not directly lead to the development of new antibiotics.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · IMPACT OF GENETICS AND GENOMICS ON MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 2)

items.subtopic: IMPACT OF GENETICS AND GENOMICS ON MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 2)
items.question: What is a significant application of recombinant DNA and RNA technology?
options: Production of peptide hormones in large amounts
Direct modification of human genes in vivo
Development of traditional small molecule drugs
items.explanation: Recombinant DNA and RNA technology is used to produce peptide hormones, growth factors, cytokines, and vaccines.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · IMPACT OF GENETICS AND GENOMICS ON MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 2)

items.subtopic: IMPACT OF GENETICS AND GENOMICS ON MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 2)
items.question: What role does the microbiome play in human health?
options: It has no significant effect on normal physiology
It only affects disease states without influencing normal physiology
It significantly impacts both normal physiology and various disease states
It is unrelated to human health
items.explanation: The microbiome significantly affects both normal physiology and various disease states.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · IMPACT OF GENETICS AND GENOMICS ON MEDICAL PRACTICE (part 2)

items.question: What is the approximate number of base pairs in a haploid human genome?
items.explanation: The human haploid genome consists of ~3 billion base pairs.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · THE HUMAN GENOME (part 1)

items.question: Which type of organism has a genome size nearly 1000-fold smaller than the human genome?
items.explanation: The E. coli genome is nearly 1000-fold smaller than the human genome.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · THE HUMAN GENOME (part 1)

items.question: What percentage of the human genome accounts for protein-coding sequences?
items.explanation: Only ~1% of the human DNA accounts for protein-coding sequences.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · THE HUMAN GENOME (part 1)

items.question: What is the nature of the genetic code in terms of codon and amino acid relationship?
options: Each codon specifies multiple amino acids
Degenerate, with most amino acids specified by several codons
items.explanation: The genetic code is degenerate; most amino acids can be specified by several different codons.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · THE HUMAN GENOME (part 1)

items.question: Which portion of a gene is spliced together to form mRNA?
items.explanation: Exons are the portions of genes that are eventually spliced together to form mRNA.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · THE HUMAN GENOME (part 2)

items.question: What percentage of the human genome does the exome constitute?
items.explanation: The exome constitutes only 1.14% of the genome.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · THE HUMAN GENOME (part 2)

items.question: What is the average frequency of single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) in the human genome?
items.explanation: SNPs occur on average every 100–300 bases in the human genome.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · THE HUMAN GENOME (part 2)

items.question: What is the primary role of noncoding RNAs such as miRNA and lncRNA?
options: They regulate gene expression and other cellular processes.
They form the structural components of chromosomes.
They serve as templates for DNA replication.
items.explanation: Noncoding RNAs like miRNA and lncRNA are involved in transcriptional and posttranscriptional regulation of gene expression, among other processes.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · THE HUMAN GENOME (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following disorders is associated with genetic abnormalities in DNA mismatch/repair?
items.explanation: Xeroderma pigmentosum is associated with defects in DNA repair mechanisms.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · THE HUMAN GENOME (part 3)

items.question: During which phase of the cell cycle does DNA synthesis occur?
items.explanation: DNA synthesis occurs during the S phase.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · THE HUMAN GENOME (part 3)

items.question: What is the result of chromosomal segregation and recombination during meiosis?
options: Reduction to haploid state in gametes
items.explanation: Meiosis results in the reduction to a haploid state in gametes.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · THE HUMAN GENOME (part 3)

items.question: Which of the following best describes the role of transcription factors in gene expression?
options: Transcription factors bind to DNA sequences and regulate transcription by activating or repressing it.
Transcription factors are responsible for mRNA processing after transcription.
Transcription factors only interact with RNA polymerase during translation.
Transcription factors modify histone proteins directly.
items.explanation: Transcription factors bind to DNA sequences and regulate gene expression by activating or repressing transcription.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · REGULATION OF GENE EXPRESSION (part 1)

items.question: Where are key regulatory elements that control gene expression often located?
options: Within the intronic regions of genes.
Only at a large distance from the proximal promoter.
In the densely packed promoter region near the transcription start site and sometimes far away in enhancers.
Exclusively within the coding sequence of genes.
items.explanation: Key regulatory elements are often located in the promoter region near the transcription start site and can also be found at a distance in enhancer regions.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · REGULATION OF GENE EXPRESSION (part 1)

items.question: What is the role of co-activators and co-repressors in gene regulation?
options: They bind directly to RNA polymerase.
They interact with DNA-binding transcription factors to form large regulatory complexes.
They are involved only in mRNA processing.
They modify the primary DNA sequence.
items.explanation: Co-activators and co-repressors interact with DNA-binding transcription factors to form large regulatory complexes that control gene expression.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · REGULATION OF GENE EXPRESSION (part 1)

items.question: What is the primary function of CpG islands in promoter regions?
options: They are typically methylated to silence transcription.
They facilitate transcription by being usually unmethylated.
They serve as binding sites for RNA polymerase.
They prevent DNA from being transcribed.
items.explanation: CpG islands in promoter regions are typically unmethylated, which facilitates transcription.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · REGULATION OF GENE EXPRESSION (part 1)

items.question: Which gene encodes a long non-coding RNA that mediates X-chromosome inactivation?
items.explanation: The Xist gene encodes a long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) that mediates gene silencing on one of the X chromosomes.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · REGULATION OF GENE EXPRESSION (part 2)

items.question: What is the primary epigenetic mechanism involved in genomic imprinting?
items.explanation: Imprinting is mediated through DNA methylation of one of the alleles.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · REGULATION OF GENE EXPRESSION (part 2)

items.question: Which syndrome results from mutations in the MECP2 gene?
items.explanation: Mutations in the MECP2 gene cause Rett syndrome, an X-linked dominant disorder.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · REGULATION OF GENE EXPRESSION (part 2)

items.question: Which epigenetic alteration is associated with the silencing of tumor-suppressor genes?
items.explanation: Hypermethylation results in the silencing of CpG islands in promoter regions of genes, including tumor-suppressor genes.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · REGULATION OF GENE EXPRESSION (part 2)

items.question: What term is recommended by several professional organizations to describe sequence changes instead of 'mutation'?
items.explanation: The more neutral term 'variant' is now recommended over 'mutation'.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: What type of mutation results in an amino acid substitution if the codon is altered?
items.explanation: A missense mutation occurs when a DNA sequence change alters an amino acid.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: Which type of point mutation introduces a premature stop codon resulting in a truncated or missing protein?
items.explanation: A nonsense mutation results in the introduction of a premature stop codon.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: What is the term for mutations that alter the reading frame due to insertions or deletions not in multiples of three bases?
items.explanation: Frameshift mutations alter the reading frame due to insertions or deletions not in multiples of three bases.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: Which type of genetic alteration is described as a variant of unknown significance (VUS)?
options: A synonymous polymorphism that alters mRNA stability.
A nonsynonymous polymorphism that changes the amino acid sequence.
A mutation with unclear functional consequences or benign variation.
An unequal crossing-over resulting in gene duplication.
items.explanation: VUS refers to a genetic alteration where it is often unclear whether it creates a change with functional consequences or is a benign variation.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: What factor contributes to the higher probability of acquiring new point mutations in the male germline compared to the female germline?
options: The number of cell divisions required for oogenesis.
Increased rates of aneuploidy in males.
Greater number of cell divisions during spermatogenesis.
Higher mutation rate per base pair in females.
items.explanation: The male germline undergoes more cell divisions (spermatogenesis involves ~30 divisions by puberty and 20 each year thereafter), increasing the probability of new point mutations.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: Which genetic disorder is caused by unequal crossing-over resulting in duplication of a gene?
options: Hereditary neuropathy with liability to pressure palsies.
items.explanation: Duplication of the PMP22 gene due to unequal crossing-over results in type IA Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: What is a consequence of gene conversion involving intergenic exchange between a gene and a related pseudogene?
options: Increased mutation rates in somatic cells.
Formation of new alleles with no functional change.
items.explanation: Gene conversion can result in nucleotide substitutions during intergenic exchange, often altering the function of the gene involved.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: Which gene is associated with congenital adrenal hyperplasia due to nucleotide substitutions that correspond to sequences in the CYP21A1P pseudogene?
items.explanation: Nucleotide substitutions in the CYP21A2 gene are associated with congenital adrenal hyperplasia.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 3)

items.question: What mechanism is suggested to explain revertant mosaicism where an inherited mutation is corrected in certain cells?
items.explanation: Mitotic gene conversion has been suggested as a mechanism to explain revertant mosaicism.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 3)

items.question: Which genetic disorder is characterized by defects in DNA damage recognition or nucleotide excision repair, leading to sensitivity to ultraviolet irradiation?
items.explanation: Patients with xeroderma pigmentosum have defects in DNA damage recognition or nucleotide excision repair.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 3)

items.question: Which gene is homologous to those involved in DNA repair and control of cell cycle checkpoints, associated with ataxia-telangiectasia?
items.explanation: The ATM gene is homologous to genes involved in DNA repair and control of cell cycle checkpoints.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 3)

items.question: Which type of mutation typically results in phenotypic alterations when a single allele is affected?
items.explanation: Gain-of-function mutations are typically dominant and result in phenotypic alterations when a single allele is affected.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 4)

items.question: What mechanism can cause variability in clinical features among patients with an identical mutation?
items.explanation: The influence of modifying genes can cause variability in clinical features among patients with an identical mutation.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 4)

items.question: Which condition is associated with haploinsufficiency affecting the expression of rate-limiting enzymes?
items.explanation: Haploinsufficiency in enzymes involved in heme synthesis can cause porphyrias.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 4)

items.question: What is the term used to describe males with a mutation in an X chromosomal gene or females with a loss of one X chromosomal locus?
items.explanation: The term 'hemizygous' is used to describe males with a mutation in an X chromosomal gene or females with a loss of one X chromosomal locus.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 4)

items.question: Which of the following diseases is associated with specific HLA haplotypes due to its proximity to the HLA locus?
items.explanation: 21-hydroxylase deficiency is associated with specific HLA haplotypes due to its proximity to the HLA locus.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 5)

items.question: What does allelic heterogeneity refer to?
options: Different phenotypes caused by mutations in different genes
The presence of multiple alleles at a single genetic locus causing similar phenotypes
Variation in phenotype due to environmental factors
items.explanation: Allelic heterogeneity refers to different mutations in the same genetic locus causing an identical or similar phenotype.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 5)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a reason for the near-random distribution of inactivating mutations on gene maps?
items.explanation: Phenotypic heterogeneity is not a reason for the near-random distribution of inactivating mutations on gene maps.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 5)

items.question: What can comprehensive sequencing technologies like WES and WGS facilitate?
options: Identification of only coding region mutations
Detection of environmental influences on gene expression
items.explanation: Comprehensive sequencing technologies like WES and WGS facilitate unbiased mutational analyses.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 5)

items.question: Which genetic disorder can result from mutations in either COL1A1 or COL1A2 genes?
items.explanation: Mutations in the COL1A1 or COL1A2 genes can cause osteogenesis imperfecta.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 6)

items.question: What is a common feature of mutations in the dystrophin gene?
options: They always result in Duchenne muscular dystrophy.
They can cause either Duchenne or Becker's muscular dystrophy, depending on severity.
They are associated with autosomal recessive inheritance.
They lead to phenocopies mimicking other genetic disorders.
items.explanation: Mutations in the DMD gene can result in either Duchenne or Becker's muscular dystrophy based on the severity of the protein defect.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 6)

items.question: What is a potential consequence of nonallelic heterogeneity in genetic studies?
options: It reduces the ability to identify disease loci in linkage studies.
It ensures that all patients with similar phenotypes have the same genetic disorder.
It eliminates the need for considering multiple genes in genetic testing.
items.explanation: Nonallelic heterogeneity can reduce the ability to identify disease loci in linkage studies due to different genetic disorders causing similar phenotypes.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 6)

items.question: What is an example of a condition that can exhibit variable expressivity?
items.explanation: Multiple endocrine neoplasia (MEN) is an example of a condition that can exhibit variable expressivity, but cystic fibrosis also shows variability in severity.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 6)

items.question: Which condition is an example of a dominant disorder with incomplete penetrance?
items.explanation: Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy (HCM) caused by mutations in the myosin-binding protein C gene is a dominant disorder with incomplete penetrance.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 7)

items.question: What factor can modify the penetrance of AHO?
items.explanation: In patients with AHO, mutations in the Gα subunit (GNAS1 gene) are expressed clinically only if inherited from their mother due to imprinting.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 7)

items.question: What is a characteristic of X-linked disorders in heterozygous females?
options: They express the phenotype fully like males.
The expression depends on the degree of X-inactivation.
They never transmit the disorder to offspring.
items.explanation: For X-linked disorders, heterozygous females may variably express the phenotype depending on the degree of X-inactivation.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 7)

items.question: What is a common cause of congenital adrenal hyperplasia?
items.explanation: Congenital adrenal hyperplasia is most commonly caused by 21-hydroxylase deficiency.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 7)

items.question: What is the probability that offspring will be affected by an autosomal dominant disorder if one parent is affected?
items.explanation: In autosomal dominant disorders, there is a 50% probability that an offspring will be affected because the mutated allele can be passed from the affected parent.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 8)

items.question: Which mechanism does NOT typically cause autosomal dominant disorders?
items.explanation: Autosomal dominant disorders are not typically caused by biallelic loss of gene function, which is more characteristic of autosomal recessive disorders.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 8)

items.question: In the context of autosomal recessive disorders, what can explain the higher frequency of certain diseases like sickle cell anemia?
options: Selective biologic advantage for heterozygous state
Increased mutation rate in affected populations
items.explanation: Certain autosomal recessive disorders occur more frequently due to a selective biological advantage for the heterozygous state, such as resistance to malaria in sickle cell anemia carriers.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 8)

items.question: What is a common feature of autosomal recessive disorders?
options: They often involve enzymes or proteins in metabolic pathways.
They are more frequent in males than females.
They result from dominant negative mutations.
items.explanation: Autosomal recessive disorders frequently involve enzymes, receptors, or proteins in signaling cascades due to a loss of function caused by mutated alleles.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 8)

items.question: Which statement best describes the inheritance pattern of X-linked disorders?
options: All daughters of an affected male are obligate carriers.
Males and females have equal risk of developing the disease.
X-linked disorders can be transmitted from father to son.
items.explanation: In X-linked inheritance, all daughters of an affected male inherit his mutant allele, making them obligate carriers.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 9)

items.question: Why are males more likely to develop the phenotype associated with a mutant X-chromosomal gene?
options: Males are hemizygous for the X chromosome.
Females do not express X-linked genes.
items.explanation: Males have only one X chromosome, making them hemizygous and more likely to express a mutant phenotype.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 9)

items.question: What is the consequence of a crossing-over event involving the SRY region during meiosis?
options: It results in XY females or XX males.
items.explanation: Crossing-over involving the SRY region can result in XY females (without SRY) or XX males (with SRY).
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 9)

items.question: Which of the following is true about mitochondrial DNA inheritance?
options: It is inherited from both parents.
It undergoes recombination like nuclear DNA.
It is transmitted through the maternal line.
Mutations in mtDNA are always inherited.
items.explanation: Mitochondrial DNA is inherited maternally, as sperm does not contribute significant cytoplasmic components to the zygote.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 9)

items.question: Which syndrome is characterized by ovarian cysts that secrete sex steroids, causing precocious puberty?
items.explanation: McCune-Albright syndrome is associated with ovarian cysts that secrete sex steroids, leading to precocious puberty.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 10)

items.question: What genetic mechanism leads to the development of Prader-Willi syndrome when there is no deletion on chromosome 15?
items.explanation: Prader-Willi syndrome can result from paternal uniparental disomy, where two maternal chromosomes are inherited.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 10)

items.question: Which gene's overactivity is involved in the pathogenesis of Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome?
items.explanation: Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome involves overactivity of the IGF-2 gene.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 10)

items.question: What is a common feature of hydatidiform moles in terms of chromosomal origin?
options: They are exclusively of maternal origin.
They have an abnormal number of chromosomes.
They are exclusively of paternal origin.
items.explanation: Hydatidiform moles contain a normal number of diploid chromosomes, but they are exclusively of paternal origin.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 10)

items.question: Which of the following best describes chromothripsis?
options: A process involving single nucleotide polymorphisms.
Multiple clustered chromosomal rearrangements in close vicinity, often after ionizing radiation injury.
A type of gene amplification seen in cancer cells.
The loss of function mutations in tumor-suppressor genes.
items.explanation: Chromothripsis involves multiple clustered chromosomal rearrangements in close vicinity, often after ionizing radiation injury.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 11)

items.question: What is the role of telomerase in cancer cells?
options: It shortens telomeres to limit cell division.
It adds telomere repeats, preventing telomere shortening and enhancing replicative capacity.
It repairs DNA damage within chromosomes.
It induces apoptosis in cancerous cells.
items.explanation: Telomerase adds telomere repeats at the ends of chromosomes during replication, preventing their shortening and thereby enhancing replicative capacity in cancer cells.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 11)

items.question: In the context of hereditary retinoblastoma, what does the two-hit model describe?
options: Two somatic mutations inactivating both alleles of a tumor-suppressor gene.
One germline mutation and one somatic mutation inactivating both alleles of a tumor-suppressor gene.
A single dominant mutation causing cancer without additional hits.
Epigenetic silencing of two different genes.
items.explanation: In hereditary retinoblastoma, one mutated RB allele is inherited (germline), and the second allele is inactivated by a somatic mutation, consistent with the two-hit model.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 11)

items.question: Which of the following best explains anticipation in nucleotide repeat expansion disorders?
options: Disease symptoms appear later in successive generations.
The number of repeats decreases, leading to milder disease over time.
Disease manifestations may worsen or occur earlier with increasing repeat length across generations.
Anticipation is unrelated to the number of nucleotide repeats.
items.explanation: In nucleotide repeat expansion disorders, anticipation refers to worsening or earlier onset of disease symptoms as repeat length increases in successive generations.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 11)

items.question: Which term describes a trait influenced by multiple genes and environmental factors?
items.explanation: A multifactorial trait is one where multiple genes interact with environmental factors.
items.reference: Principles of Human Genetics · TRANSMISSION OF GENETIC DISEASE (part 12)
